cards.question: The sources of light which have been made by man are called man-made sources of light. Example- electric bulb, tube-light, torch, etc.
cards.text_html: The sources of light which have been made by man are called man-made sources of light. Example- electric bulb, tube-light, torch, etc.

cards.question: The sources of light which occur in nature as such are called natural sources of light. Example- sun and stars.
cards.text_html: The sources of light which occur in nature as such are called natural sources of light. Example- sun and stars.

cards.question: Non-luminous objects: The objects which do not give out their own light are called non-luminous objects. Examples - table, chair, trees, flower-pot, a pencil, a car, etc. The non-luminous objects can be seen because they reflect light into our eyes.
cards.text_html: Non-luminous objects: The objects which do not give out their own light are called non-luminous objects. Examples - table, chair, trees, flower-pot, a pencil, a car, etc. The non-luminous objects can be seen because they reflect light into our eyes.

cards.question: Luminous objects: The objects which give out their own light are called luminous objects. Examples - sun, stars, burning candle, lighted torch, etc.
cards.text_html: Luminous objects: The objects which give out their own light are called luminous objects. Examples - sun, stars, burning candle, lighted torch, etc.

cards.question: Rectilinear propagation of light: Light takes the quickest path between any two points. Therefore, light travels in a straight line. This is known as a rectilinear propagation of light.
cards.text_html: Rectilinear propagation of light: Light takes the quickest path between any two points. Therefore, light travels in a straight line. This is known as a rectilinear propagation of light.

cards.question: Light as a form of energy Light is the energy that enables us to see. Light is emitted from a source such as the Sun.
cards.text_html:
Light as a form of energy

  • Light is the energy that enables us to see.
  • Light is emitted from a source such as the Sun.

cards.question: Source of light: An object which emits light, is called a source of light. For example, sun, torch, etc.
cards.text_html: Source of light: An object which emits light, is called a source of light. For example, sun, torch, etc.

cards.question: Transparent materials: The materials which allow all the light to pass through them are called transparent materials. For example- glass, polythene, air, water, etc.
cards.text_html: Transparent materials: The materials which allow all the light to pass through them are called transparent materials. For example- glass, polythene, air, water, etc.

cards.question: Translucent objects are those objects which allow only some of the light to pass through them. Examples - butter paper, tissue paper, oiled paper, muddy water, etc.
cards.text_html: Translucent objects are those objects which allow only some of the light to pass through them. Examples - butter paper, tissue paper, oiled paper, muddy water, etc.

cards.question: The materials which do not allow any light to pass through them are called opaque materials. Examples- stone, cardboard, book, wooden door, etc.
cards.text_html: The materials which do not allow any light to pass through them are called opaque materials. Examples- stone, cardboard, book, wooden door, etc.

cards.question: Image Shadow 1. Has the color of the object. 1. Is always black, regardless of the colour of the object. 2. Gives the details as well as the outline of the object. 2. Give only an outline of the object. 3. Undergoes lateral inversion. 3. Does not undergo lateral inversion.
cards.text_html:
Image Shadow
1. Has the color of the object. 1. Is always black, regardless of the colour of the object.
2. Gives the details as well as the outline of the object. 2. Give only an outline of the object.
3. Undergoes lateral inversion. 3. Does not undergo lateral inversion.

cards.question: Shadow: An area of darkness formed by an opaque object obstructing light is called a shadow. An opaque object stops the light completely, so it casts a dark shadow behind it.
cards.text_html: Shadow: An area of darkness formed by an opaque object obstructing light is called a shadow. An opaque object stops the light completely, so it casts a dark shadow behind it.

cards.question: A translucent object casts a weak shadow because it stops the light partially.
cards.text_html: A translucent object casts a weak shadow because it stops the light partially.

cards.question: A transparent object does not cast any shadow because it does not stop any light passing through it.
cards.text_html: A transparent object does not cast any shadow because it does not stop any light passing through it.

cards.question: We require three things to observe a shadow: A source of light An opaque object A screen on which the shadow can be seen
cards.text_html:
We require three things to observe a shadow:

  1. A source of light
  2. An opaque object
  3. A screen on which the shadow can be seen

cards.question: The shadow of an object has the following characteristics: The shadow of an object is erect. The shadow of an object is real. The shadow is always black in colour. The shadow can be smaller than the object, equal to the object or bigger than the object.
cards.text_html:
The shadow of an object has the following characteristics:

  1. The shadow of an object is erect.
  2. The shadow of an object is real.
  3. The shadow is always black in colour.
  4. The shadow can be smaller than the object, equal to the object or bigger than the object.

cards.question: A pinhole camera works on the principle that light travels in straight lines.
cards.text_html: A pinhole camera works on the principle that light travels in straight lines.

cards.question: The pinhole camera consists of a closed box having a small pinhole in the front and a translucent screen made of butter paper at the back which acts as a screen to receive the image of an object. The pinhole camera is used to view the images of various objects like trees and buildings, etc.
cards.text_html: The pinhole camera consists of a closed box having a small pinhole in the front and a translucent screen made of butter paper at the back which acts as a screen to receive the image of an object. The pinhole camera is used to view the images of various objects like trees and buildings, etc.

cards.question: Formation of image by pinhole camera A pinhole camera is a simple camera that consists of a light-proof box, a thin film for a screen and a small aperture or hole to allow the passage of light rays. The light from outside enters through the small hole and forms an image on the screen that is inverted.
cards.text_html:
Formation of image by pinhole camera

  • A pinhole camera is a simple camera that consists of a light-proof box, a thin film for a screen and a small aperture or hole to allow the passage of light rays.
  • The light from outside enters through the small hole and forms an image on the screen that is inverted.

cards.question: Characteristics of the image formed by a pinhole camera: The image in a pinhole camera is real and inverted. The image in a pinhole camera is of the same colour as the object. The image in a pinhole camera can be smaller than the object, equal to the object or bigger than the object depending on the distance of object from the pinhole camera.
cards.text_html:
Characteristics of the image formed by a pinhole camera:

  1. The image in a pinhole camera is real and inverted.
  2. The image in a pinhole camera is of the same colour as the object.
  3. The image in a pinhole camera can be smaller than the object, equal to the object or bigger than the object depending on the distance of object from the pinhole camera.

cards.question: Mirrors A mirror is a surface usually consisting of a glass that reflects light incident on it to form clear erect images.
cards.text_html:
Mirrors
A mirror is a surface usually consisting of a glass that reflects light incident on it to form clear erect images.

cards.question: Reflection When light is incident on a surface, it gets reflected or it bounces back. Any surface that is really well polished or shiny acts like a mirror. The phenomenon of light bouncing off surfaces is called reflection.
cards.text_html:
Reflection
When light is incident on a surface, it gets reflected or it bounces back. Any surface that is really well polished or shiny acts like a mirror. The phenomenon of light bouncing off surfaces is called reflection.

cards.question: Characteristics of images Images have colour, unlike shadows. They are formed due to the converging rays of light that comes after reflecting from objects. A real image is formed by actual convergence of light rays. Real images always form on a screen. A virtual image is the apparent convergence of diverging light rays. Virtual images cannot be obtained on a screen.
cards.text_html:
Characteristics of images

  • Images have colour, unlike shadows. They are formed due to the converging rays of light that comes after reflecting from objects.
  • A real image is formed by actual convergence of light rays. Real images always form on a screen.
  • A virtual image is the apparent convergence of diverging light rays. Virtual images cannot be obtained on a screen.

cards.question: Plane mirrors and images formed by them A plane mirror changes the direction of light that falls on it.
cards.text_html:
Plane mirrors and images formed by them
A plane mirror changes the direction of light that falls on it.
